pattern: Sorting the lengths and testing candidate longest sides from largest to smallest means every side shorter than the current candidate is automatically a legal partner, so the first candidate that passes wins the biggest possible perimeter.
hints: For a fixed longest side, using every other available shorter side only ever helps, since adding more length to the 'other sides' total can only make the polygon inequality easier to satisfy.
Sort the lengths ascending. Starting from the very largest value as a candidate longest side, check whether the sum of everything smaller than it beats it.

If the sum of all smaller sides does not exceed the current candidate, that candidate can never be used as the longest side of any polygon here -- drop it and try the next-largest value as the new candidate, carrying the running sum forward.

edge_inputs.description: three equal sides always form a polygon
one side is far larger than the sum of the rest
mixed sizes where the largest two must be dropped before a valid triangle appears
minimum allowed length, exactly three sides
largest allowed side value alongside two minimal sides that cannot support it
every side is degenerate: two smaller sides sum to exactly the largest, which is not strictly greater
many equal sides guarantee a polygon using all of them
removing just the single largest outlier reveals a valid polygon among the rest

stress.description: large random array across the full value range, mixing feasible and infeasible shapes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large array built from many small clustered sides plus a few huge outliers
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(n - 3)]
    nums.extend([rng.randint(500000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(3)])
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    return [nums]
